coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots doped ZnO superstructure (ZnO superstructure/GQDs) for weak UV intensity photodetector application
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2020 Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l.
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Haihua
Ding, Junsong
Yang, Dingyu
Li, Jitao
Shi, Yihan
Zhou, Yuyao
coredata.dc:description: Usually, the photoresponse performances of zinc oxide based ultraviolet (UV) photodetector can be enhanced by improving the nanostructure of zinc oxide. Here, we demonstrate a kind of weak ultraviolet intensity photodetector which bases on ZnO superstructure doped with graphene quantum dots (ZnO superstructure/GQDs). The ZnO superstructure includes a ZnO nanoflower on the top of ZnO nanorod. The nanoflower has large specific surface area that is favor to the absorption of weak UV light, and the nanorod provides a good route for carriers transport. Also, the GQDs can assist the absorption of UV and the transport of carriers. The photo-response performance of the ZnO superstructure/GQDs we measured is obtained at a working bias of 3 V and an irradiate condition of 50 μW/cm2, under the excitation wavelength 365 nm. The results show that ZnO superstructure/GQDs has a low dark current (40 nA) and a good photocurrent (2.1 μA) in a low irradiation and power supply condition, and we calculate the current ratio of on/off is about 52.5 times. Further, we discuss in-depth the synthesis mechanism of ZnO superstructure and the transport mechanism of photo-generated carriers in this paper. At the same time, we develop a UV signal acquisition and voltage conversion system, which proves the practical application reference of ZnO superstructure/GQDs for weak UV intensity photodetector.
coredata.dcterms:subject: ZnO superstructure
Graphene quantum dot doping
Weak ultraviolet detector
Practical application

Synthesis of GQDs doped ZnO superstructure
The possible mechanism for ZnO superstructure synthesis
ZnO superstructures/GQDs for UV detector application

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD spectra of GQDs doped ZnO superstructure.
SEM images; (a) the total outline of sample, (b) the one-dimensional nanorods; (c) the nanoflower structure composed of two-dimensional nanosheets.
The TEM and HRTEM images of architectures; (a,b) for nanosheet, and (c,d) for nanorod; (e,f) the STEM elemental mapping images of the sample for nanosheet and nanorod, respectively.
(a) XPS spectrum of the sample; (b) high-resolution XPS spectrum of Zn2p; (c) high-resolution XPS spectrum of C1s; (d) high-resolution XPS spectrum of O1s.

It shows the synthetic reaction of process; (a) when pH value < 9, sample is growing nanorod; (b) when the pH value gradually increases, the non-growth ions are combined with the polar plane; (c) when 9 < pH value < 11, it is in the MTP stage; (d) when pH value > 11, sample is growing nanosheet.
Simulation results of ZnO crystal under coplanar electrode operating condition.
The process of photo-generated electron-hole transmission.
(a) The I–V characteristic of sample; (b) the Dark current of the sample; (c) sample's response at a bias voltage of 3 V and an irradiation of 50μW/cm2at the wavelength of 365 nm.
Response of device under different On-off speed; (a) light off (500 ms) - light on (500 ms; (b) light off (100 ms) - light on (100 ms); (c) light off (60 ms) - light on (60 ms).

(a) The UV signal acquisition and voltage conversion system in practice, corresponding to (b) the schematic flow chart. (c–e) The signal on the oscilloscope of the device; (c) the signal of light On-Off (60 ms-60 ms); (d) the rise time of the device; (e) the fall time of the device; the rise time and recover time for our device is 10 ms and 15.6 ms, respectively.
The detailed parameter of ZnO seed layer deposition process.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots doped ZnO superstructure (ZnO superstructure/GQDs) for weak UV intensity photodetector application

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Atmospheric Science, Chengdu University of Information Technology, Chengdu, 610225, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Atmospheric Science, Chengdu University of Information Technology, Chengdu, 610225, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Usually, the photoresponse performances of zinc oxide based ultraviolet (UV) photodetector can be enhanced by improving the nanostructure of zinc oxide. Here, we demonstrate a kind of weak ultraviolet intensity photodetector which bases on ZnO superstructure doped with graphene quantum dots (ZnO superstructure/GQDs). The ZnO superstructure includes a ZnO nanoflower on the top of ZnO nanorod. The nanoflower has large specific surface area that is favor to the absorption of weak UV light, and the nanorod provides a good route for carriers transport. Also, the GQDs can assist the absorption of UV and the transport of carriers. The photo-response performance of the ZnO superstructure/GQDs we measured is obtained at a working bias of 3 V and an irradiate condition of 50 μW/cm2, under the excitation wavelength 365 nm. The results show that ZnO superstructure/GQDs has a low dark current (40 nA) and a good photocurrent (2.1 μA) in a low irradiation and power supply condition, and we calculate the current ratio of on/off is about 52.5 times. Further, we discuss in-depth the synthesis mechanism of ZnO superstructure and the transport mechanism of photo-generated carriers in this paper. At the same time, we develop a UV signal acquisition and voltage conversion system, which proves the practical application reference of ZnO superstructure/GQDs for weak UV intensity photodetector.

ce:section.ce:para: Zinc oxide (ZnO) is a semiconductor with a wide direct bandgap of 3.37 eV and an exciton binding energy of 60 meV at room temperature that belong to group II-V [1–3]. Because ZnO nanomaterial has surface and interface effects, quantum size effects and macro quantum tunneling effects, it exhibits novel physical and chemical properties that many other materials do not possess, also ZnO nanomaterial exhibits excellent performance in optical, electricity and magnetism. So, it has an extremely high application values in sensing, field emission and other fields. Especially, the ultraviolet (UV) photodetector have received special attention in the fields of military, environmental protection and medicine in recent years [4–14]. Compare with todays’ UV photodetector, ZnO has a lot of advantages, such as, environmental friendliness, high spectral consistency, simple preparation and low cost. ZnO may be the first choice of material for UV photodetector in the future [15–19]. However, the high recombination rate of photo-generated electron-hole pairs of the pure ZnO nanomaterial and surface defects seriously decrease the efficiency of the photo-response, limiting its application. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are fragments of single or several layers (generally, less than five layers) of graphene on a nanoscale. Graphene quantum dots have a great number of advantages, such as well performance in bio-compatibility, extremely fast electron transfer rate and excellent mechanical strength. Also, it has a large specific surface area. In general, if graphene quantum dots have a particle diameter less than 100 nm, it will have a narrow band gap and a crystal nucleus structures similar to that of inorganic semi-conductor quantum dots. Moreover, it will have a good response to both ultraviolet light and visible light. GQDs have a unique edge effect. Thanks to the carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on the surface, they enhanced the absorption properties of the materials. Finally, improve the optical properties of materials [20–22].

This paper proposes a novelty ZnO superstructure composed of one-dimensional ZnO nanorod and -three-dimensional nanoflower. To the best of our knowledge, the ZnO superstructure we prepared is rare in previous reports. We found that the superstructure has a good performance under low UV irradiation intensity and bias. The nanoflower in ZnO superstructure has a large effective area, which can absorb significantly the weak UV light. Meanwhile, high crystallinity nanorod is an excellent way to transport the photo-generated electron-holes. In addition, we used graphene quantum dots to dope in ZnO superstructure and build ‘freeway’ for photo-generated electron-hole pairs. Allowing photo-generated electron-hole pairs separate more effectively and reduce their recombination rates. We used COMSOL software to simulate the built-in electric field of the thin film under the working condition of coplanar electrode, explaining the generation of response and the transmission mechanism of photo-generated electron-hole pairs. On the other hand, we have built related amplification and signal processing circuits according to the above research results, so that the conversion of the prepared UV photodetector from photocurrent to voltage can be truly applied.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The preparation of ZnO seed layer
ce:section.ce:para: Synthesis of GQDs doped ZnO superstructure was two steps. First, the ZnO seed layer that we prepared by using a RF magnetron sputtering system (JGP-450, Sky Technology Development) on ITO glass (Shenzhen Leaguer Optronics Co., Ltd.). Before the seed layer preparation, the ZnO target (purity of 99.99%, Dumpers New Materials Inc.) of 110 mm in diameters and 6 mm in thickness was pre-sputtered for 5 min to remove the impurity. We used Argon (Niuruide Inc.) as the sputtering gas which of purity 99.99%. Table 1 shows that the detailed parameter of sputtering process. After that, seed layer was annealed at 550 °C in the air for 2 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of GQDs doped ZnO superstructure
ce:section.ce:para: Second, this is hydrothermal reaction process. C6H12N4 (hexamethylenetetramine, purity of 99.0%, Aladin-reagent. Co., Ltd) and Zn(NO3)2 (purity of purity of 99.0%, Aladin-reagent. Co., Ltd) were used to prepare a solution with a concentration of 0.1 mol/L. Then the 45 ml of the prepared solution was stirred with 5 ml graphene quantum dots (Tanfeng Technology. Inc) for 30 min. The stirred solution was poured into a polytetrafluoroethylene autoclave, and the ITO glass (ITO is also coplanar electrode with channel area of 0.3 cm2) with ZnO seed layer was adhered to the top of the autoclave by double-side tape (the seed layer was toward to the liquid). After 2 h at the reaction temperature of 90 °C, the samples were washed with absolute ethanol and deionization water.

X-ray diffraction spectra (XRD, D/max-r A, Rigaku) using a Cu Ka (0.1541 nm) were recorded to analysis the crystal phase and the lattice constant of the sample. The surface micrographs of the sample are observed by scanning electron microscope (SEM, FEI Inspect F). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Supra Axis, Krotos/SHIMADZU) using an Al/Ag Ka/La (1486.6 eV/2984.3 eV) target to analysis the chemical state of the sample. Moreover, the electrical properties and device's response are measure by Agilent B2912A. The oscilloscope and power supply are Tektronix TDS2014C and Keithley 2231A-30-3, respectively. The Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM, JEM 2100F, JEOL) and Elemental Mapping (X-MaxN 80T, Oxford instruments) in order to make sure the GQDs doped into the ZnO architecture successfully.

The XRD pattern of GQDs doped ZnO superstructure is shown in Fig. 1 . In the XRD pattern, we can find that the simple has a good agreement with the JCPDS card (No. 36–1451) for the typical wurtzite-type ZnO crystals, it is shown that the addition of GQDs do not significantly change the structure. We can find that the diffraction peaks of the sample are sharp and also with a narrow FWHM which indicating that the sample has a good crystallinity. We calculate and obtain the lattice constants of the sample: a = 3.2461 Å and c = 5.19441 Å.

Fig. 3e-f are the elemental mapping images of the sample, belonging to the nanosheet and nanorod, respectively. According to the distribution of zinc, oxygen and carbon elements shown in figures in detail, we can confirm that the graphene quantum dots have been doped into the ZnO architecture successfully.

We further performed XPS analysis of the sample, as shown in Fig. 4 . We can find that graphene quantum dots have been well doped into the sample. Then, we scan the zinc, carbon and oxygen for high resolution energy spectrum. Fig. 4b shows the high-resolution XPS spectrum of Zn2p. We find that the binding energy of Zn2p3/2 and Zn2p1/2 are 1021.5 eV and 1044.6 eV, respectively [23], indicating that zinc is present in the sample essentially as a single Zn2+. However, the stander blinding energy of Zn2p3/2 and Zn2p1/2 is 1020 eV and 1042.3 eV relative to the perfect ZnO crystal. This phenomenon actually due to the interaction and electron transfer between zinc atom and the carbon atom of the graphene quantum dots, which leads to the obvious shift of the XPS spectrum peak of Zn2p. Fig. 4c is XPS spectrum of carbon. We can fit this spectrum based on 4 characteristic peaks, which are 284.4 eV, 285.2 eV, 287.5 eV and 289 eV, respectively. They represent C–C bonding, C–O bonding, CO bonding and O–CO bonding, respectively. The C–C bond stand for the graphitized carbon of sp2. The other three bonds represent that the carbon atoms of the graphene quantum dots are combine to the oxygen atoms in the ZnO in a bonding manner of C–O and CO [24–27]. In the high-resolution energy spectrum of O1s (Fig. 4d), we can fit it into two characteristic peaks. They are 531.2 eV and 521.7 eV, respectively. According to other researchers’ works, we can know that the two peaks represent the bonding of Zn–O and C–O. These spectra show the GQDs have been well doped in the ZnO superstructure, which has a high consistent with the XRD pattern.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The possible mechanism for ZnO superstructure synthesis
ce:section.ce:para:
Three-dimensional ZnO nano-superstructure usually requires a uniform ZnO crystal nucleus, so we used RF magnetron sputtering system to complete the preparation of ZnO seed layer. During the next hydrothermal reaction, the chemical equations for the reaction are shown in the following.
i
C6H12N4+6H2O→6COH2+4NH3
ii
NH3+H2O→NH4 ++OH−
iii
Zn2++OH−→Zn(OH)+
iv
Zn2++2OH−→Zn(OH)2
v
Zn2++3OH−→Zn(OH)3 -
vi
Zn2++4OH−→Zn(OH)4 2-
vii
Zn(OH)4 2−→ZnO + H2O+2OH−

Firstly, we used COMSOL software to simulate the electric field distribution of a non-defect, un-doped and uniform ZnO crystal under coplanar electrode operating condition. Because the photo-generated electron-hole pairs cannot enter the air through the surface of the ZnO, but are limited to intra-crystal transmission, so we set the model parameters as shown in Table 2 .

In the truth, our sample is doped with graphene quantum dots. It is well known that graphene quantum dots have extremely excellent electrical conductivity. The incorporation of graphene quantum dots as construction personnel that transforms ‘highway’ into ‘freeway’, promotes the transmission of photo-generated electron-hole pairs. Meanwhile, the nanoflowers have a large specific surface area that enhances the absorption of UV light, making the best use of weak UV light intensity.

To ensure the stability and correctness of our ultraviolet detector, the gold electrode must be in ohmic contact with the device. Fig. 8 a plots current-voltage (I–V) characteristics of the device with a voltage of -3 V–3 V. The device exhibits obvious a rectification ratio of 1, indicating that the gold electrode is in ohmic contact with the ZnO sample. Meanwhile, we tested the dark current of the device under a bias of 3 V and 25 °C (Fig. 8b). We linearly fit the dark current plot and find that the device has a very low dark current of 4 × 10−8 A. Then, we make a comparison to ZnO thin film reported in our previous work [29]. Our previous work have shown that the dark current of the ZnO thin film reaches 1.75 × 10−7 A under the same bias of 3 V, but the ZnO superstructure/GQDs represents a lower current, which is at least lower than 23 times. A lower dark current is due to the good crystallization, and has an advantageous (low noise) for detecting low intensity UV signals. The reason by which can detect weak signal is the flower structure of ZnO has a large number of nanosheets with thin thickness and large area, which can increase the efficiency of ultraviolet absorption. Moreover, after the ultraviolet light penetrates the surface ZnO nanosheets, the layered structure can absorb the penetrated ultraviolet light at a second time. Moreover, thanks to an extremely excellent crystallinity causes a low number of defects which can reduce the recombination of the photo-generated electron-hole pairs.

The photo-response performance of the ZnO superstructure/GQDs was tested at a bias voltage of 3 V and an irradiation of 50 μW/cm2 (Irradiation area: 0.3 cm2) at the wavelength of 365 nm. Firstly, the sample was stored in a camera obscura at room temperature for a sufficient amount of time to reduce dark current. Then, the sample received enough exposure under the experimental conditions described above to produce a saturated photocurrent. Sample's response is shown in Fig. 8c. We can find that the current from 4 × 10−8 A increase to 2.1 × 10−6 A (the calculated photocurrent is also about 2.1 × 10−6 A). The ZnO nanoflower in ZnO superstructure can increase the effective irradiation area. This device can be more fully used to generate the electron-hole pairs than other device under the same intensity of UV light source in the same time. On the other hand, the intensity limitation of UV signal light is lower intensity than others, thus suggesting that it is suitable to be applied for the low intensity signal detection. In addition, we calculated the external quantum efficiency (EQE, 48%), responsivity (R, 140 mA/W), current ratio of on/off (CR, 52.5) and detectivity (D, 3.5 × 107) of the device [30–32]. formulas (1-4) for these parameter calculations are as follows:
(1)
E Q E =
I ph
/ e
P /
(
hv × 1.6 ×
10
− 19
)
(2)
R =
I ph
P
where
I
p h
is photocurrent, e is the elementary charge, P means the irradiation power and hv = 1240/λ (λ is the wavelength of irradiation) belongs to the photon energy.
(3)
C R =
I ph
I d
(4)
D =
CR P
where
I d
belong to dark current before the irradiation.

We further explore the transient response of ZnO superstructures/GQDs. After a series of experiments, we find that the detector still performs well under the illumination conditions of 365 nm wavelength, light on (500 ms) - light off (500 ms) (Fig. 9 a) to light on (100 ms) – light off (100 ms) (Fig. 9b). However, when we further increase the switching frequency, it is found from Fig. 9c that under the light on (60 ms) – light off (60 ms) illumination conditions, the signal has become unstable. We find that a certain amount of ripple is generated in the signal. The reason is as follows.

We can make a discovery in that the ripple in the signal has a high regularity. After a lot of further experiments, we find that this phenomenon is due to the fact that the sensitivity of the device we prepared does not match the shutter of the light source. Because when the shutter device performs an excessively fast switching action, due to the effect of inertia, it will cause a certain amount of rebound, resulting in a change in light intensity. On the other hand, the response speed of our detectors is much faster than the switching speed of the shutter. Finally, the mechanical instability of the shutter causes ripple in the signal. We are able to detect the faint details of the shutter activity, which also indirectly represents the high responsiveness of our detector.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ZnO superstructures/GQDs for UV detector application
ce:section.ce:para: In order to show the practical application value of ZnO superstructures/GQDs, we develop a UV signal acquisition and voltage conversion system, as shown in Fig. 10 . The two coplanar ITO electrodes of sample link to the ports “2” and “3” of part A, respectively. Part A serves as the conversion of UV light to photocurrent signal. Then the photocurrent out from port “4”enters part B through the port “5”. Here, the photocurrent is changed to the voltage signal and output to the oscilloscope via port “9”. The voltage signal will be processed further, such as digital analog switching, when it is applied in practice.

For the reason that nowadays commercial detectors are basically voltage-type devices, so that we have designed amplifier circuits and signal processing circuits. The original current signal is converted and amplified into a voltage signal. Finally, on the oscilloscope, we can obtain the voltage signal in Fig. 10c-e, suggesting that ZnO superstructures/GQDs can be applied in practice.

In this work, ZnO superstructure/GQDs hybrid architectures is prepared by two steps, including the first ZnO seed layer prepared by RF magnetron sputtering and the final sample prepared by hydrothermal reaction. ZnO superstructure consists of ZnO nanorod and ZnO nanoflower on the top of nanorod. The ZnO superstructure/GQDs shows good UV photo-response performances such as low dark current and good responsivity under a low intensity of irradiation and bias. According to XRD and SEM, we find that the ZnO superstructure/GQDs has an extremely high crystallinity. Thanks to this reason, the sample expresses a very low dark current. On the other hand, the ZnO superstructure we have prepared can greatly increase the effective irradiation area, due to its large specific surface area induced by the nanoflower structure. So, it has a higher photocurrent even under a low intensity irradiation conditions and bias. In addition, we have conducted in-depth research in the synthesis mechanism of this structure. We proposed the concept of morphological transition point (MTP) for the explanation of the synthesis mechanism, which is mainly determined by the pH value of the hydrothermal solution and the polarity of the surface of the ZnO. In order to comprehend the transmission mechanism, we simulated the electric field distribution in the device based on COMSOL software. The results show that the photo-generated electron-hole pairs are generated in the nanoflower and nanorod. Then, they transmitted along the nanorod and finally separated in the seed layer to obtain signal. Meanwhile, because of the addition of GQDs which play a role of electron accelerator, the response speed of the sample is improved. On this foundation, we design the relevant amplifying circuit, so that the device can be practically applied in real-world scenarios. We believe that it has the predictable application prospect both in military and civilian applications.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC) No. 11675029 and program from Department of Science and Technology of Sichuan Province No. 2018JY0453, and project of Education Department of Sichuan Province No. 2018Z073.
